title: Neglected Peers in Merger Valuations
abstract: Using novel merger valuation data, we show that firms selected by investment banks as “comparable peers” are more than twice as likely to later become takeover targets themselves compared to matched control firms. Peer firms not subsequently acquired attract more institutional ownership and analyst coverage, deliver strong operating performance, reduce investments, and increase payouts. Investors are inattentive, though, to peer identification at the time of merger filings’ public disclosure. A portfolio that longs peers and shorts controls earns up to 15.6%alpha annually, which mainly comes from the long leg and is difficult to explain by short-sale constraints.

article.1. Institutional Background Empirical Pr:
JEL G14 - Information and Market Efficiency; Event Studies; Insider Trading G34 - Mergers; Acquisitions; Restructuring; Corporate Governance G41 - Role and Effects of Psychological, Emotional, Social, and Cognitive Factors on Decision Making in Financial Markets Issue Section: Article Editor: Lauren Cohen Lauren Cohen Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Drawing on hand-collected data about investment bank valuation taken from firms’ merger documents filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), this paper investigates (1) whether the valuation information disclosed in merger filings has implications for asset returns and mechanisms driving returns and (2) whether and how the financial market incorporates this information when it is publicly disclosed. Answers to these questions offer a new perspective on both corporate finance issues and asset pricing phenomena in the debate over the usefulness of investment bank valuation (e.g.,DeAngelo 1990;Elson 1992;Cain and Denis 2013;Liu 2020), the argument over the informativeness of corporate filings (e.g.,Brown and Tucker 2011;Cohen, Malloy, and Nguyen 2020), and the unsettling finding of a very weak relation between information arrival and stock price movement (see the reviews ofHirshleifer 2001;Tetlock 2014).

We exploit an institutional requirement that provides one type of disclosure in merger filings that could be informative for stock returns and corporate outcomes, yet investors will likely neglect this particular disclosure because of the unobvious utility and complexity of the information found therein. In an acquisition of a publicly traded U.S. firm, the merging parties are required to file merger documents with the SEC. The information disclosed in these lengthy documents is voluminous.1If the target firm hires an investment bank to provide a valuation analysis, which is almost always the case nowadays, the bank’s valuation, called a “fairness opinion,” must be included in the merger filing. The information content of the bank’s valuation and the market’s reaction to this disclosed information are the focus of this paper.

Comparable companies analysis (CCA) is one of the most popular valuation techniques in investment banking. Because CCA derives an estimate of a firm’s value from peer firms’ valuation multiples, banks must select comparable firms and perform a valuation of these peers to evaluate the target firm.Eaton et al. (2022)investigate banks’ incentive effects and the value implications of the selected peers in the offer negotiations of mergers and acquisitions (M&As). They find that target firms’ investment banks strategically select large peers with high valuation multiples, factors that are positively related to takeover premiums. While Eaton and coauthors examine how peer selection is related to transaction outcomes, we instead focus on the subsequent performance of the peers themselves.

On the one hand, the documented high peer valuation multiples raise the bar for these peer firms to outperform in later periods. On the other hand, high multiples do not necessarily indicate overvaluation, if they are indeed justified by better growth opportunities and strong fundamentals. Moreover, peers could outperform for several reasons. First, a peer firm comparable to the target firm can be attractive to other bidders and thus may have a higher probability of becoming a takeover target itself in the future. Second, peer firms may experience an increase in institutional ownership after the deal’s completion because some of the target firm’s previous investors will likely switch to investing in a comparable firm. The same argument applies to analyst coverage. Higher institutional ownership and analyst coverage can also lead to increased investor recognition. As a result, peer firms may outperform, given the substantial premiums paid in acquisitions and the positive relation between stock returns and increases in institutional ownership, investor recognition, and coverage (e.g.,Sias, Starks, and Titman 2006;Lehavy and Sloan 2008;Richardson, Sloan, and You 2012;Lee and So 2017).

To test these possibilities, we identify a nonpeer control firm from the same industry and of a similar size for each peer firm selected by an investment bank. We begin by examining whether peers have strong fundamentals to justify their high valuation multiples and find that this is in fact the case. Compared with the target firm, selected peers have significantly better operating performance, higher return to investments, and better credit profiles. Moreover, these peer firms are significantly more likely to become takeover targets in the following few years relative to control firms. For example, 8.3%–19.5%of peers become targets in the next 12–36 months, while 3.7%–10.0%of control firms become targets during that same time.

Next, we examine whether and how the market incorporates the value-relevant information of being identified as peers by M&A advisors when merger filings are publicly disclosed. We start with the short-run returns around the time of the merger filing disclosure. We find that both peer firms and control firms experience no price reaction at the time of merger filing.Hirshleifer and Teoh (2003)argue that evidence from short-window event studies does not answer the question of whether information is fully incorporated into prices; we therefore investigate long-run abnormal returns to peer firms and control firms. We find that peers significantly outperform the market while nonpeers closely track market initially but slightly underperform by the end of the 24 months subsequent to the disclosure of merger filings.

We also employ alternative asset pricing models to test peers’ long-run returns. A portfolio formed at the end of the filing month that longs peers and shorts control firms earns a statistically significant 0.46%alpha per month (5.5%per year), based on the six-factor model (⁠t=4.96⁠), over the following year. Moreover, the abnormal return comes almost entirely from the long leg, which is difficult to explain by short-sale constraints. Annual alphas can reach over 15%in subsample analyses. We interpret these results as being consistent with theoretical studies predicting that, because of the limited attention investors can pay to multiple sources of information, they ignore value-relevant yet nonsalient information, leading to large-return predictability (e.g.,Hirshleifer 2001;Hirshleifer and Teoh 2003;Peng and Xiong 2006).

Next, we investigate how much the takeover channel contributes to peers’ alphas. We show that, not surprisingly, peers that become targets themselves produce massive alphas. Moreover, peers remaining independent outperform as well, thus indicating that the takeover channel alone does not fully explain peer outperformance. We conduct further analyses of change in ownership and coverage, separately for the target and nontarget subsamples. We find that, consistent with our expectations, peers that do not become targets experience significant increases in institutional ownership, analyst coverage, and investor recognition. These peers deliver stronger operating performances measured by return on assets and gross margin. They also reduce investments and increase total payouts in the post-filing periods. These results suggest that peers that do not become targets are more successful companies with strong operating performance and better governance, which contributes to their abnormal returns in the post-filing periods. However, the much-delayed price responses indicate an underreaction by investors to the information disclosed in merger filings.

To provide more direct evidence of investor inattention, we examine merger filing downloads from the SEC’s Electronic Data Gathering and Retrieval (EDGAR) website and a news search from Bloomberg. We followBen-Rephael, Da, and Israelsen (2017)and construct a Bloomberg attention variable to capture institutional investor attention. We find that investor attention spikes on the merger announcement date, but not on the filing date. Furthermore, no abnormal institutional attention is paid to peer firms when merger filings are publicly disclosed. Further analyses suggest that analysts also miss the peer information when revising their forecasts around the filing month.

We perform a number of additional investigations and robustness checks. We find two possible channels through which peers are more likely to become targets: banks’ informational advantages and their ability to facilitate later transactions. Our results are not sensitive to alternative industry classifications with or without size restrictions when choosing control firms, the inclusion of additional risk factors, exclusion of tender offers, alternative ways to cluster standard errors, or alternative model specifications.

Our paper contributes to the literature in several ways. First, our results contribute to the literature investigating the likelihood of a public firm becoming a takeover target. The review article byEckbo (2014)highlights the difficulty of predicting takeover targets. Eckbo reports that, in an average year, roughly 5%of U.S. publicly traded firms are acquired. Despite the typically substantial premiums paid to target shareholders, predicting targets with any degree of accuracy has proven challenging (e.g.,Palepu 1986;Cremers, Nair, and John 2009). Our results suggest that investors largely miss a source of value-relevant information useful for identifying future takeover targets. Our findings also shed light on the mechanisms by which peers become targets themselves.

Second, our results speak to the role of investment bank valuation in the M&A literature. The literature records a long-lasting debate over whether investment bank valuations in fairness opinions contain any useful information (e.g.,Bebchuk and Kahan 1989;DeAngelo 1990;Elson 1992;Cain and Denis 2013). Critics claim that fairness opinions are biased and uninformative because they lack objectivity given the contingent fee structure and conflicts of interest. The significantly higher likelihood of peers becoming targets and the robust return predictability, however, provide strong evidence suggesting that investment bank valuations do contain informative content.

Third, our paper is related to the fast-growing literature that employs textual analysis to extract information from corporate filings. The bulk of prior studies focuses onperiodicdisclosures, such as 10-K or 10-Q filings.2Our paper extends this literature by providing novel evidence thattransactionalfilings in takeovers contain rich information on a target firm’s closest competitors. Yet investors appear largely to miss the implication of this credible information for asset prices. Therefore, our results speak to the debate over the informativeness of corporate filings (e.g.,Brown and Tucker 2011;Cohen, Malloy, and Nguyen 2020) and the effect of investor inattention on asset prices (Cohen and Frazzini 2008;Cohen, Malloy, and Nguyen 2020).

Finally, our findings add to the literature on anomalies. Prior studies have demonstrated an asymmetry in the ease of buying versus the greater difficulty of shorting because there is less arbitrage capital available to short overpriced stocks than to purchase underpriced stocks. As a result, short-leg profits usually contribute a large portion of the documented anomalous returns on assets (Stambaugh, Yu, and Yuan 2012;Stambaugh and Yuan 2017). Our paper adds to this literature by identifying return predictability driven by the long leg of underpriced stocks that are large, liquid, and have high analyst coverage. Thus, our results indicate that inattentive investors’ neglect of information can cause mispricing even for high-profile, well-performing firms. Our results connect the corporate finance literature with the asset pricing literature by showing that information production in the corporate world has significant implications for asset returns, and asset returns, in turn, shed light on important issues in corporate finance.
1.1 Fairness opinions and investment bank valuation

In the M&A process, investment banks play a central role by providing advisory services, getting involved in negotiations, and providing a valuation analysis, known as a fairness opinion. Fairness opinions have been a ubiquitous feature of takeover transactions since the Delaware Supreme Court’s decision inSmith v. Van Gorkomin 1985.3One implication of the decision was that a target firm’s board, as part of its duty of care in a corporate control transaction, was now obligated to inform itself on its corporation’s sale value by securing a well-prepared financial analysis (Davidoff 2006).

Empirical studies show that since the ruling inVan Gorkom, fairness opinions have become widely used as a way to help target boards make informed decisions.Cain and Denis (2013)report that, in their sample, nearly every target firm had at least one fairness opinion in a negotiated merger.Liu (2020)finds that over 98%of target firms obtained fairness opinions in a sample of firms from 1996 to 2013.4In our own sample, about 96%of target firms obtained fairness opinions. The ubiquity of fairness opinions in M&As reduces any potential selection concern over a firm’s choice to get a fairness opinion from an investment bank.

Fairness opinions presented to corporate boards and disclosed in merger documents typically provide details of the investment bank’s valuation analysis in arriving at an opinion on the financial fairness of the proposed transaction. The board of directors then uses the estimated valuation range to negotiate the offer price and merger terms. The direct costs to obtain a fairness opinion from an investment bank average between $500,000 and $750,000.Figure 1illustrates a timeline for obtaining fairness opinions and filing merger documents during an M&A.5

The investment bank typically uses multiple methods to estimate the value of the target firm. The two most common and accepted ones are discounted cash flow analysis and CCA (Davidoff, Makhija, and Narayanan 2011;Liu 2020). While the discounted cash flow approach focuses on the target firm’s intrinsic value from its projected future cash flows, CCA is an external valuation that derives the target firm’s value from a valuation of selected comparable peer firms.Liu (2020)reports that over 90%of investment banks use both valuation methods.Gompers, Kaplan, and Mukharlyamov (2016)show that private equity investors rely on CCA even more than on discounted cash flow.6

Although banks use CCA as their primary valuation technique in practice, their valuation details are not always disclosed. The CCA disclosure requirements depend on the form of the deal (e.g., merger vs. tender offer). If the transaction is structured as a merger, the target firm must disclose fairness opinion valuation details in its proxy statement. In contrast, tender offer rules do not explicitly require this disclosure. Therefore, target firms may decide whether or not to provide valuation details in their tender offer filings.7

1.2 Peer selections and empirical predictions

Eaton et al. (2022)conducted one of the first studies investigating investment banks’ incentive effects and the value implications of selected peers for takeover premiums. The authors hypothesize that banks’ incentives could work in two opposite directions: banks may choose peers to aid in the negotiation of a higher offer price for target firm shareholders or choose peers that help with the completion of a proposed deal. The incentive to select peers in a way that helps the target negotiate upward with a prospective bidder implies that the target’s investment bank chooses peers that, all else being equal, have higher valuation multiples, which make the perceived value of the target firm appear higher. Conversely, the incentive to select peers in a way that expedites the closing of a transaction (and the bank’s collection of its advisory fees) implies that the investment bank chooses peers that, all else being equal, have lower valuation multiples. Consistent with the bargaining channel, the authors find that target firms’ investment banks strategically select large, high-growth peers with high valuation multiples, factors that are positively related to takeover premiums.

The results reported inEaton et al. (2022)indicate that banks’ incentives do affect their peer choices. The implication of this choice for peer firms’ subsequent performance is, however, not immediately clear. On the one hand, the high valuation multiples suggest that these peers are not cheap, which heightens the hurdle they must clear to outperform in later periods. On the other hand, high multiples do not necessarily indicate overvaluation, if indeed they are justified by the company’s growth opportunities and strong fundamentals.

In merger negotiations, a target’s advisors have incentives to bargain for a higher price for their clients, which helps them build a reputation for selling firms at high premiums. However, banks also have incentives to maintain good relationships with acquirers because acquiring firms are the surviving entities that could hire them in the future for services in other transactions, such as acquisitions and securities underwriting. Maintaining relationships with acquirers would be difficult if banks intentionally chose overpriced peers for negotiation and these peers’ stock prices were later to drop significantly.8We therefore hypothesize that banks are more likely to select high-multiple peers with strong fundamentals and growth potential.

Another main finding inEaton et al. (2022)is that banks are skilled at selecting peers in the same business sector and with similar products, services, and technologies. This finding has several implications for peer firms’ subsequent performances. First, a peer firm comparable to the target firm can be attractive to other bidders and thus may have a higher probability of becoming a takeover target itself in the future. Prior literature shows that regulatory and technological shocks touch off merger waves in an industry, and capital liquidity causes industry mergers to cluster in time (Mitchell and Mulherin 1996;Harford 2005). However, even within the same industry, firms are not likely to have an equal probability of becoming a takeover target. The fact that the target company in an announced M&A deal is viewed as comparable to several selected peers indicates that, even within the same industry, these selected peers (compared to nonchosen peers) are more likely to attract potential bidders.

Moreover, peer firms may experience an increase in institutional ownership due to takeover-induced demand. When the target firm is delisted at deal completion, the existing institutional investors of the target firm are likely to switch to a similar firm, especially if the merger consideration is cash. After cashing out, institutional investors may need to rebalance their portfolios by investing in firms that are comparable to the target firm. Higher institutional ownership may also lead to increased investor recognition. Likewise, we expect some of the analysts who previously covered the takeover target firm to switch to covering a comparable firm after the deal’s completion, given their industry expertise.

Overall, we expect peer firms to have strong fundamentals, a higher likelihood of becoming a takeover target, and increased institutional ownership, investor recognition, and analyst coverage in subsequent periods. Given the substantial amount of premiums paid in acquisitions and the positive relation between returns and increases in institutional ownership, investor recognition, and analyst coverage (e.g.,Sias, Starks, and Titman 2006;Lehavy and Sloan 2008;Richardson, Sloan, and You 2012;Lee and So 2017), we expect peers firm to outperform in subsequent periods.

1.3 Related literature on market reactions to industry peers and predicting targets

Prior studies show that industry rivals of acquisition targets typically earn significantly positive returns around the initial announcement of the target firm’s acquisition (Eckbo 1983;Eckbo 1985;Mitchell and Mulherin 1996;Song and Walkling 2000). Two potential explanations exist for the positive returns of industry rivals: the collusion hypothesis and the acquisition probability hypothesis. The literature generally rejects the collusion explanation and favors the acquisition probability hypothesis. For example,Song and Walkling (2000)show that the magnitude of returns to rivals is positively related to the probability of being a target.

Accurately predicting takeover targets, however, is challenging. The review article byEckbo (2014)reports that despite the typically substantial premiums paid to target shareholders, predicting targets with any degree of accuracy has proven to be difficult.Dodd and Ruback (1977)show that the stock market does not identify takeover targets with a high degree of accuracy;Palepu (1986)concludes that it is difficult to predict targets after correcting for methodological flaws present in earlier studies. Using a panel of 83,000-plus firm-year observations and including all observable firm-specific characteristics, such as growth opportunities, capital, and ownership structure,Cremers, Nair, and John (2009)report that the explanatory power of their regression model is only around 3%⁠. Our analyses show that banks’ peer selection can be particularly useful in predicting future takeover targets.

1.4 Related literature on market reactions surrounding corporate filings

A growing number of studies investigate the information content and asset return implications of firms’ regular reports in 10-Q and 10-K filings (e.g.,Cohen and Frazzini 2008;You and Zhang 2009;Brown and Tucker 2011;Lee 2012;Dyer, Lang, and Stice-Lawrence 2017;Ertugrul et al. 2017;Cohen, Malloy, and Nguyen 2020;Drake et al. 2020). Particularly Cohen et al. provide intriguing evidence that simple changes to firms’ annual reports contain rich information. Given the risk of failing to report negative information, changes to annual reports predict large negative returns in the future. However, stock prices exhibit little reaction at the time of public filing, with the negative information impounded into prices with significant delay.

In contrast to the growing literature investigating firms’ regular filings, the information content and return implications of firms’ transactional filings have received limited attention among academic researchers. Related to the initial public offering process,Loughran and McDonald (2013)find that offerings with higher uncertainty words in Form S-1 have higher first-day returns, absolute offer price revisions, and subsequent volatility.Boone, Floros, and Johnson (2016)find that firms experience greater underpricing when Form S-1 contains more redacted information. Most recently,Liu et al. (2022)find significant market reactions to filing amendments in deals with SEC comment letters.

In the M&A setting,Cain and Denis (2013)test bidder abnormal returns surrounding the date on which the proxy is posted on the EDGAR website. The authors conclude that investment banks produce information not previously available to market participants by rendering target-side fairness opinions. Our paper differs from Cain and Denis’s in that we focus on market reactions to selected comparablepeerfirms instead of themergingfirms.

1.5 Related literature on investor attention around M&As

Prior studies have shown that M&A announcements generate a significant amount of investor and media attention.Liu and McConnell (2013)report, on average, about five news stories appear in the 10 days around an M&A announcement.Ahern and Sosyura (2014)find that bidders increase news media reports surrounding M&A events. Despite the importance of the merger announcements,Louis and Sun (2010)report lower abnormal trading volumes for acquirers’ merger announcements and less pronounced acquirer announcement returns if the deals are announced on Friday, consistent with evidence that investors are distracted from work-related activities on Fridays (DellaVigna and Pollet 2009).

In the M&A context, selected peer firms are not “event” firms. The selection of peers is a by-product of investment banks’ valuation of the takeover target. Around an M&A announcement, investor attention naturally goes to the featured bidder and target because the merging parties are the focus of the media coverage. In addition to the nonsalience of information on peer firms compared with the merging firms, the information on peers is typically buried in a small section of the merger document, which often runs hundreds of pages long.

Appendix B provides an example of the location of the information on peer firms in the fairness opinion of a merger document. The deal was announced in 2000, and the target firm was Ben & Jerry’s. The partially reproduced table of contents shows that the fairness opinion starts on page 21 of the document. In the fairness opinion section, the paragraph headed “Comparable company analysis” is toward the end of the section. The names of the six peers are found in the last sentence of this paragraph.

Given the amount of information and the detailed nature of the analysis, it is not difficult to imagine that investors ignore this information or may not even be aware of its existence, despite the possibility that it could have important implications for the peers’ future stock returns. Theoretical studies predict that inattentive investors’ neglect of information depends on the news’s salience, availability, and ease of processing (e.g.,Hirshleifer 2001;Hirshleifer and Teoh 2003;Peng and Xiong 2006). We contend that investigating peer firms in M&A valuation provides an ideal setting for testing not only the informational relevance of banks’ peer selection but also the market’s reaction to relevant yet nonsalient information.

As shown in panel A ofTable 1, among the 5,596 merger documents we collect from EDGAR, we identify 5,355 (95.6%⁠) deals where target firms obtained fairness opinions from their advisors. About 90%(4,808) of these deals mention the use of CCA as a valuation technique. However, 824 deals (the majority of them being tender offers) do not disclose peer identities.11Our final sample contains 3,984 deals that disclose at least one comparable company in the “Opinion of financial advisor(s)” section.

Among those 3,984 deals, we identify 40,506 peer firm names. For each peer, we match the peer’s name with company names in the Compustat database. We have 4,529 unique peer names that cannot be definitively matched, so we attempt to manually match those peers’ names with Compustat company names. We are able to identify 3,137 firms of the 4,529 unmatched firms. We then merge the peer data with the CRSP/Compustat database, resulting in 35,287 peer firms with available stock and accounting information.12

For each peer firm in our sample, we find a matched control firm with the same two-digit Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) code and of a similar size.13After removing peer firms that cannot be matched, our final sample consists of 32,682 pairs of comparable companies and matched control firms. In our main tests, we first compare peers’ likelihood of becoming targets themselves with that of matched nonpeer firms, and controlling for industry and size helps us ensure that these factors do not drive any differences in our results. We then implement the event study methodology to investigate short-term returns and the calendar-time portfolio approach to examine long-term returns to peers and matched nonpeers.

We acknowledge the potential limitations of using SIC codes. As discussed inHoberg and Phillips (2010,2016), a static membership classification, such as SIC, does not capture the time-varying product market location of a firm and the time-varying identity of its rivals. Using textual analyses of 10-K filings and the “cosine similarity” method, Hoberg and Phillips develop a novel measure of text-based network industries, which captures product and industry changes over time. We rely on SIC codes to identify control firms because it is a simple and convenient reference for investors in forming their portfolios and implementing trading strategies. As robustness checks, in Section 7.4, we explicitly examine the role of product market space using the flexible text-based network approach, as well as using the propensity score matching technique, to identify alternative control groups.

2.2 Summary statistics

Panel B ofTable 1presents the distribution of peer firms and the number of deals by year. We report the total number of peer firms, the number of unique peer firms, and the number of target firms each year. We observe the highest number of peer firms selected in comparable companies analyses from the late 1990s, due to the documented merger wave in this period (e.g.,Andrade, Mitchell, and Stafford 2001;Harford 2005). We also observe a fairly large number of peer firms selected during 2005–2007 due to a leveraged buyout boom (Kaplan and Stromberg 2009). Overall,Table 1, panel B, shows that we have a fairly large number of peer firms in our portfolio construction.

Panel C ofTable 1reports the characteristics of target firms, peer firms, and control firms. Column 4 presents tests of differences in medians between peer firms and target firms. We report median comparisons of firm characteristics to show that the results are not driven by outliers. Consistent withEaton et al. (2022), we find that peers are twice to three times as large as the target firms based on all three size measures (i.e., total assets, market capitalization, and enterprise value). Peers also have significantly higher valuation multiples relative to the target firms, using either the market-to-book (M/B) ratio or enterprise-value-to-EBITDA (EV/EBITDA).

Next, we compare fundamental performance between the target firms and selected peers prior to merger announcements. For operational performance metrics, we compute sales growth, operating cash flow, return on assets (ROA), profitability (gross profit margin, EBITDA margin, EBIT margin, and net income margin), earnings per share, and credit profile (EBITDA-to-interest coverage ratio and EBIT-to-interest coverage ratio). Consistent with our expectations, we find strong evidence that, compared with target firms, peer firms have significantly better operating performance by all measures. These results suggest that peers’ higher valuation multiples relative to target firms could be justified by their better growth opportunities and strong fundamentals.

Column 5 of panel C compares peer firms with control firms. Despite our effort to identify control firms of a similar size, we still find statistical differences between these two groups. Peer firms have slightly higher total assets and market capitalization, and slightly lower enterprise value. For example, the median market capitalization of peers is $738 million, while for control firms, it is $690 million. It is worth noting that peer firms and control firms are both large firms, given that the median market capitalization among all firms in the CRSP/Compustat database in the same sample period is $240 million. Compared to control firms, peers have a lower M/B and EV/EBITDA ratio. Regarding operating performance, peers have lower sales growth and EBITDA margin but have better operating cash flow, ROA, gross profit margin, and credit profiles.

3.1 Likelihood of peers becoming a takeover target subsequently

As discussed in Section 2.2 , peers deemed to be comparable to the target firm may themselves be attractive targets for takeover in the future. We test this hypothesis by estimating the following regression model: Target = β 0 + β 1 Peer + β 2 log(AT) + β 3 B/M + β 4 Prior return + β 5 Ivol + β 6 Illiquidity + β 7 Sales growth + β 8 ROA + β 9 Leverage + β 10 Cash holding + ε , (1) where the dependent variable, Target , is an indicator variable that equals one if firm i becomes a takeover target in the next 12, 24, or 36 months, and zero otherwise. 14 The key independent variable, Peer , is an indicator variable that equals one if the firm is selected by the investment bank as a comparable firm disclosed in the merger document filed in month t ⁠ , and zero for the control firm. 15 We follow Bena and Li (2014) and include firm characteristics as control variables. We also include deal fixed effects to control for unobservable deal characteristics. 16 Appendix A defines all variables.

Panel A ofTable 2reports summary statistics on the likelihood of peers and nonpeers becoming targets. On average, 8.3%of selected peers become targets themselves in the subsequent 12 months, compared to 3.69%of nonpeers from the same industry. 3.2 Market reactions

3.2.1 Short-term return analysis

In this subsection, we investigate whether the market reacts to the value implication of firms being identified as peers by investment banks in M&A valuations. Panel A ofTable 3shows the average cumulative abnormal return (CAR) for both peer firms and control firms over the window of (⁠−1, 1) and (⁠−10, 1) days surrounding the disclosure of merger documents, where day 0 is the merger filing date. Abnormal returns are returns adjusted for market returns from the value-weighted CRSP index. We find no obvious market reaction to either peer or control firms when the merger document is publicly released. The averagePeer CARover the 3-day window (11-day window) around merger filing disclosure is 0.02%(0.1%⁠), and the averageNonpeer CARis−0.03%(0.06%⁠).

The insignificant market reaction to the release of merger filings could be consistent with two potential explanations. The first explanation is the investor inattention hypothesis. Suppose investors direct their attention mostly to the merging firms (i.e., the takeover target and the bidder). In that case, they may miss value-relevant information disclosed in the merger documents, which are lengthy and complex. This first explanation implies market inefficiency.

In contrast, an alternative explanation could be that the market is efficient enough to have already incorporated the information even before the public disclosure of merger filings. Prior studies show that industry rivals of acquisition targets experience positive returns around deal announcements due to an expectation that industry peers will later become targets (e.g.,Song and Walkling 2000).17We therefore investigate returns to peers and control firms around the initial deal announcements.

Panel B ofTable 3reports that, consistent with prior literature, both peers and nonpeers have significantly positive returns around deal announcements. The averagePeer CARover the 3-day window around merger announcement is 0.54%⁠, and the averageNonpeer CARis 0.22%⁠; both are statistically significant. In addition, the difference is also statistically significant, suggesting that peers experience even higher returns over the (⁠−1, 1) window surrounding the deal’s announcement. However, when we expand the event window to include potential price run-up or market speculations immediately before the merger announcement, we find that the difference in abnormal returns between peers and nonpeers becomes much smaller and insignificant.Peer CARover the window (⁠−10, 1) surrounding the merger announcement is 0.82%(⁠t=14.4⁠), andNonpeer CARis 0.72%(⁠t=11.9⁠).

3.2.2 Long-term return analysis

Hirshleifer and Teoh (2003)argue that evidence from short-window event studies does not resolve the question of whether there is under- or overreaction in the initial response. We therefore conduct a long-term return analysis to complement our short-term event study. We start our long-term analysis by investigating whether peer firms can systematically beat the market in the subsequent periods. We plot post-filing market-adjusted returns (CARs) for peers and matched control firms separately inFigure 2. Panel A shows that peers reliably outperform the market by about 9%in the 24 months following the filing month. In contrast, control firms perform much like the market initially but later underperform the market by about 1.0%toward the end of the 24 months. Panel B shows that the difference in stock returns between these two groups is as large as 10%and is highly statistically significant (⁠p<.001⁠).

One potential concern is that peer firms’ higher returns post-filing might just be a continuation of their outperformance preceding the deal. If this were the case, then this group’s superior performance post-filing could be a momentum effect instead of an effect of bank selection. We address this issue in two ways. First, we conduct a pre-trend analysis to investigate return differences between peers and control firms before the merger filing month. Second, we formally test how peers and control firms perform using asset pricing factor models that explicitly include the momentum factor.

Panel C ofFigure 2plots the cumulative return differences between peers and control firms from 6 months before the release of banks’ peer selection to 2 years after disclosing the peers. We find that peers slightly underperform relative to control firms in the months leading up to the disclosure of banks’ peer selection. The cumulative return difference between peers and control firms reaches the lowest point (⁠−2.69%⁠, statistically significant at the 1%level) in month−1, which is the month immediately before the merger filing month. Peers then start to outperform control firms in the merger filing month (i.e., the negative return difference starts to shrink). The return difference reaches zero around month 4, becomes positive, and continues to increase after that.18The observation that peers start to outperform in the month that their identities are disclosed (but not before) is consistent with banks’ choice of peers revealing valuable information about post-filing returns. However, this information is not fully incorporated into prices when the merger filing is publicly released. Instead, it is incorporated into prices very slowly.

Next, we test whether commonly known risk factors can explain higher returns to peer firms. To perform this test, we compute standard calendar-time portfolios for peer firms and control firms. Stocks enter the portfolios at the end of the filing month (seeFigure 1for the timeline of portfolio construction). If a firm gets acquired, the stock is dropped from the portfolio after the acquisition and delisting. Portfolios are rebalanced monthly. We present the average monthly return inTable 4. Panel A presents equal-weighted calendar-time portfolio returns for a 3-month holding period.19The peer-control portfolio that longs peers and shorts control firms earns 42 basis points per month (⁠t=3.96⁠). This result is unaffected by using different asset pricing models: capital asset pricing model (CAPM), theFama and French (1993)three-factor model, the Carhart four-factor model (Fama and French 1993;Carhart 1997), theFama and French (2015)five-factor model, and the six-factor model including the five Fama-French factors and the momentum factor.

Overall, the results reported inTable 4show that the higher returns to peers do not reflect temporary overpricing because the alphas are strong and persistent and do not reverse. 3.3 How much does the takeover channel contribute to the outperformance of peers?

Having shown that peer firms are significantly more likely to become targets, which can be a primary channel through which peer firms experience higher returns, we next investigate how much this channel contributes to peers’ abnormal returns by splitting the peer group into subsamples based on whether the peer becomes a target in the 1-year window following the filing month. We calculate the calendar-time portfolio returns for these subsamples separately and report the results inTable 5. For the 8.3%of peers that become takeover targets, the monthly alphas range from 3.02%to 3.59%⁠, equivalent to 36.2%to 43.1%annually. We note that, ex ante, we do not have information on which 8.3%of peers will actually become targets; thus, the annual alpha of more than 30%cannot be achieved. Our purpose, however, is to illustrate how much the takeover channel contributes to the overall outperformance of peer firms.

However, based on the four-, five-, and six-factor models, the remaining peers that do not become takeover targets still earn significant alphas. Overall,Table 5shows that peers becoming targets substantially outperform, and peers not becoming targets still outperform in terms of future alphas.22Therefore, the takeover channel provides some, but not a complete, explanation of peer outperformance.
article.4. Further Investigation of the Mechanis:
In this section, we examine other potential channels for value creation in addition to the takeover channel.

4.1 Increase in institutional ownership, investor recognition, and analyst coverage

As discussed in Section2.2, we expect peer firms to experience an increase in institutional ownership, investor recognition, and analyst coverage, all of which are documented to be positively related to stock returns (e.g.,Sias, Starks, and Titman 2006;Lehavy and Sloan 2008;Richardson, Sloan, and You 2012;Lee and So 2017). To test these channels, we use an empirical approach similar to what we employed for testing the likelihood of becoming a takeover target, except that we use the change in institutional ownership, change in investor recognition, and the change in analyst coverage as our dependent variables instead. We run these tests separately for firms that become targets (i.e., the target subsample) and for firms that remain independent (i.e., the nontarget subsample).

Panel A ofTable 6reports the regression results for a change in institutional ownership. We find a significant increase in institutional ownership for peer firms in the nontarget subsample, as the coefficient forPeeris highly significant across all columns. Specifically, after controlling for all firm and deal characteristics, being selected as a peer to a target firm is associated with an increase in institutional ownership of 1.1%(2.1%or 3.1%⁠) in the next 12 (24 or 36) months. In contrast, among the takeover subsample, being selected as a peer is not significantly associated with an increase in institutional ownership in the subsequent periods.

Panel B ofTable 6reports regression results for change in investor recognition. FollowingLehavy and Sloan (2008), we use the change in the proportion of 13-F filers holding a particular security as an empirical proxy for the change in investor recognition.23Similar to the results for changes in institutional ownership, we find a significant increase in investor recognition for peer firms in the nontarget subsample but insignificant results among the target subsample.

Panel C ofTable 6reports regression results for change in analyst coverage. We again find that, among the nontarget subsample, peers experience a significant increase in analyst coverage across all columns. For the takeover target subsample, we find some evidence that being selected as a peer is associated with an increase in analyst coverage in the 3-year period, although the magnitude of the coefficient is much smaller compared to the nontarget subsample (0.188 vs. 0.364).

Overall,Table 6’s results show robust evidence of increases in institutional ownership, investor recognition, and analyst coverage for peer firms, particularly among the nontarget sample. The literature has documented a positive relation between contemporaneous stock returns and changes in institutional investor ownership and investor recognition (e.g.,Sias, Starks, and Titman 2006;Lehavy and Sloan 2008;Richardson, Sloan, and You 2012). Moreover,Lee and So (2017)associate analyst coverage with stock returns by showing that firms with high analyst coverage subsequently outperform firms with low coverage. Our interpretation of the results displayed inTable 6is that while the substantial takeover premiums mainly contribute to their outperformance of the peer firms becoming targets, peers that do not become takeover targets benefit from increases in institutional ownership, investor recognition, and analyst coverage.

We are cautious about inferring a causal relation between banks’ peer choice and an increase in institutional ownership, investor recognition, and analyst coverage because our results do not suggest that having an investment bank randomly choose a peer firm for its fairness opinion would bring that firm higher institutional ownership and coverage. Rather, our results suggest that banks are good at identifying comparable peer firms on account of their expertise and industry knowledge. Moreover, banks tend to name peers with strong fundamentals in their fairness opinions. Therefore, being chosen by a bank as a peer contains credible information on the peer’s comparability to the target (and, by extension, the likelihood of it also becoming a takeover target), the peer’s fundamentals, and its future operating performance, which we investigate in the next subsection.

4.2 Post-filing operating performance and corporate policy

Here, we examine peer firms’ post-filing operating performance and firm policy to help pin down the mechanism. Prior studies show that both institutional investors and analysts play an important governance role and that increased institutional ownership and analyst coverage are associated with improved monitoring (e.g.,Chung and Jo 1996;Gillan and Starks 2000;Yermack 2010;Chen, Harford, and Lin 2015;Iliev et al. 2015). Moreover, the improved governance from investors’ monitoring is associated with reduced investments and increased payouts (Iliev, Kalodimos, and Lowry 2021), and increased analyst coverage is associated with improved fundamental performance and reduced acquisitions (Chen, Harford, and Lin 2015;Lee and So 2017).

We first examine changes in operating performance for peers and nonpeers inTable 7. The dependent variables are changes in ROA and gross margin. Similar toTable 6, we run the tests separately for the target subsample and for the nontarget subsample. Panel A ofTable 7reports the regression results for change in ROA. We find a significant increase in ROA for peer firms in the nontarget subsample, as the coefficient forPeeris highly significant across all columns. In contrast, we find that among the takeover subsample, being selected as a peer is not significantly associated with an increase in ROA in the subsequent periods. Panel B ofTable 7reports results for changes in gross margin. We show that among the nontarget subsample, peers experience significantly higher increases in gross margin relative to control firms. Again, these results are insignificant for the target subsample. We further investigate anormal returns around earnings announcements to test whether peer return performance concentrates around the revelation of operating performance. FollowingSloan (1996), we decompose total abnormal returns into abnormal returns around earnings announcements and the nonannouncement period returns inTable IA4in theInternet Appendix. We find that for the nontarget sample during the 1-year period post-filing, over 21%of the total abnormal returns are concentrated around the subsequent quarterly earnings announcements, even though the announcement period only contains 4.8%of the total trading days (12 of 252). The results are insignificant for the target subsample.

The main independent variable, Peer , is an indicator variable that equals one if a firm is a peer firm, and zero otherwise. In panel A, the dependent variable is change in ROA ( Change in ROA ), calculated as a difference in mean quarterly ROA in the 1-, 2-, or 3-year period post-filing versus prefiling. Columns 1 to 3 report results for the subsample of firms that remain independent during the 1-, 2-, or 3-year period post-filing. Columns 4 to 6 report results for the subsample of firms that become takeover targets during the 1-, 2-, or 3-year period post-filing. ROA and change in ROA are in percentages. In panel B, we repeat the regression analysis by replacing the dependent variable with change in gross profit ( Change in gross margin ). We control for the predisclosure level of gross margin (i.e., Pre gross margin ) in the regressions. Appendix A defines all variables. t -statistics with heteroscedastic-robust standard errors are reported in parentheses. * p < .1 ⁠ ; ** p < .05 ⁠ ; *** p < .01 ⁠ .

We then investigate corporate policies, measured by changes in investments, total payout, and financing, inTable 8. Panel A reports the regression results for change in investment. FollowingLehavy and Sloan (2008), we measure investments as capital expenditure plus acquisitions, less depreciation and sales of property, plant, and equipment, scaled by total assets. We find that, relative to control firms, peer firms invest less in the subsequent periods. These results are again concentrated among the nontarget subsample.

In panel B, we investigate changes in total payout, measured as dividends plus repurchases, scaled by total assets. Results show that for the nontarget subsample, peers significantly increase total payouts relative to control firms in the post-filing periods. Among the target subsample, the coefficients forPeerare positive but insignificant. Overall, these results are consistent with improved monitoring leading to reduced investments and increased payouts (Iliev, Kalodimos, and Lowry 2021). These results are also consistent with the asset pricing literature showing a negative relation between investment growth and returns, and a positive relation between total payouts and returns (e.g.,Titman, Wei, and Xie 2004;Fama and French 2006;Boudoukh et al. 2007).

Panel C examines changes in financing. We find that relative to control firms, peers have reduced cash flows from financing activities. The financing results are significant among both the nontarget and the target subsamples. Both reduced investments and increased payouts are likely to contribute to the reduced cash flow from financing activities.

Overall,Tables 7and8provide further evidence corroborating that peers firms, especially those do not become takeover targets, appear to have strong operating performance and governance, which contribute to their return performance in the post-filing periods.
article.5. Investor Attention/Inattention:
In this section, we explore whether investor inattention is likely to explain the nonreaction around the release of merger documents and the subsequent abnormal returns to peer firms.

5.1 Evidence from EDGAR merger filing downloads

We start by examining merger filing downloads from the SEC’s EDGAR website by nonrobot users. We follow the literature and remove robot downloading because prior studies show a lack of intelligence for these bulk downloaders (e.g.,Lee, Ma, and Wang 2015;Loughran and McDonald 2017).24We capture investors’ information-acquisition activity in EDGAR by examining daily merger filing downloads.25To act on the information about peer firms from a bank’s valuation, investors would likely need to download the merger filing. Other information sources, such as news coverage, may report some merger information but are highly unlikely to cover peer selection from the comparable companies analysis.

Figure 3, panel A, plots the median EDGAR downloads for corporate filings of the merging firms in the 5 days surrounding the merger announcement date and the filing date. Within 2 days following the merger announcement, the median EDGAR downloads for regular corporate filings is 82 (40 on day 0 and 42 on day 1). Given that the median download is around five the week before the merger announcement, the substantial increase in downloading reflects heightened investor attention when the merger is announced.

Figure 3 Investor attention around merger announcement and filing This figure plots investor attention measured by EDGAR download requests and Bloomberg news search. Panel A displays the median of EDGAR download requests from nonrobot users around the merger announcement (Ann.) date and the merger document filing date. Gaps between announcement period segments and filing period segments are caused by differences in time-to-filing across mergers. We plot median download requests from 5 days prior to announcement date (filing date) to 5 days after. The solid line traces median download requests for regular corporate filings filed by the target or the acquirer. The dashed line traces merger documents filed by the target or the acquirer. The dashed line with markers traces corporate filings filed by peer firms. Panel B plots Bloomberg attention around the merger announcement date and the merger document filing date. We construct the Bloomberg attention measure following Ben-Rephael, Da, and Israelsen (2017) . Bloomberg records the number of times news articles on a particular stock are read by its terminal users and the number of times users actively search for news for a specific stock. Bloomberg then assigns a value of one for each article read and 10 for each news search. These numbers are then aggregated into an hourly count. Using the hourly count, Bloomberg then creates a numerical attention score each hour by comparing the past eight-hour average count to all hourly counts over the previous month for the same stock. Bloomberg assigns a value of zero if the rolling average is in the lowest 80 % of the hourly counts over the previous 30 days. Similarly, Bloomberg assigns a score of one, two, three, or four if the average is between 80 % and 90 % ⁠ , 90 % and 94 % ⁠ , 94 % and 96 % ⁠ , or greater than 96 % of the previous month’s hourly counts, respectively. Finally, Bloomberg aggregates up to the daily frequency by taking a maximum of all hourly scores throughout the day. Gaps between announcement period segments and filing period segments are caused by differences in time-to-filing across mergers. We plot median Bloomberg attention from 5 days prior to announcement date (filing date) to 5 days after. The solid line reflects median Bloomberg attention by the target or the acquirer. The dashed line with markers reflects median Bloomberg attention by peer firm. The sample period is from 2003 to 2017 for panel A and is from 2010 to 2017 for panel B. Open in new tab Download slide

Relative to the merger announcement, investor attention level is much reduced around the merger filing date. Within 2 days following the filing date, the median download for regular corporate filing (excluding merger documents) is 18 (12 on the filing date and 6 on the following date). Moreover, for the newly released merger filings that contain investment bank valuation and peer firm information, the median download is 12 on the filing date and 10 on the following date. These results suggest that investors pay more attention to the merger announcement due to the salience of the M&A news. In contrast, the much-smaller attention around the filing date suggests that investors consider little “news content” in the later released merger documents, since the merger has already been announced and most merger-related information (such as offer price and other merger terms) is already known.

5.2 Evidence from Bloomberg news search

A potential caveat for using EDGAR downloads as a proxy for investor attention is that this measure misses investors accessing merger filings through third-party vendors, such as Capital IQ or Bloomberg Terminal. This could be problematic, especially if more sophisticated institutional investors do not use EDGAR as their main information source. To alleviate this concern, we followBen-Rephael, Da, and Israelsen (2017)and construct a Bloomberg attention variable to capture institutional investor attention. This measure captures the news search and news reading activity for stocks on Bloomberg terminals, which are used primarily by institutional investors.

Bloomberg records the number of times each article is read and searched for a particular stock. Because actively searching for news requires much more attention than passively reading news, Bloomberg assigns a higher weight when users search for news than read a news article. To construct the attention measure, for each stockjon dayt⁠, Bloomberg aggregates user hourly search activity and assigns an attention score (ranging from zero to four) to stockjon daytby comparing investor search behavior on daytwith the search behavior during the previous 30 days. Given that the Bloomberg attention score captures investor attention on a particular day relative to the previous 30 days, a score of zero indicates no abnormal attention, and a score of four indicates a spike in abnormal attention.26

Figure 3, panel B, plots the median Bloomberg attention for the target or the acquirer in the 5 days surrounding the merger announcement date and the filing date. We observe that institutional attention to the target firm and the acquiring firm spikes at the merger announcement date. The median Bloomberg attention score increases to the highest level of four on the announcement date, from zero on the previous day. The search attention declines substantially the day after the deal announcement and reverts to zero the following day. On the merger filing date, the attention score increases to one, suggesting slightly increased investor attention paid to the merging firms when merger documents are disclosed. These patterns suggest that institutional investors also pay more attention to the merger announcement than to the merger filing.

5.3 Searching for peers around the filing date

If investors are attentive to peer information disclosed in the merger documents, then we expect the attention paid to peer firms to increase at the filing. We thus investigate EDGAR download activities and Bloomberg news search on the filing date forpeerfirms. Panel A ofFigure 3plots the median EDGAR downloads for the corporate filings of peers. We find that on the date the merger filing is released, the median peer download is five, which is comparable and slightly higher than the day before the release of merger documents (when the median download is four). Moreover, peer filing download requests are much lower compared to the merging firms on the same day.

Panel B ofFigure 3shows that the Bloomberg attention index for peer firms is zero on days before the merger filing date and remains zero on the merger filing date. Overall,Figure 3suggests that the M&A announcement attracts much more investor attention compared to the release of transaction filings. Moreover, attention to peer firms does not seem to increase at the time their identities are disclosed in the merger documents.

5.4 Analysts’ forecasts surrounding the release of merger filings

Prior studies have characterized security analysts as an important intermediary for information dissemination and have documented that both individual and institutional investors rely on analysts’ forecasts to make investment decisions (e.g.,Hong, Lim, and Stein 2000). We thus investigate how analysts’ behavior changes surrounding the release of merger filings. If sophisticated information users, such as security analysts, incorporate information disclosed in the merger document, we would expect them to raise their price target forecasts or recommendations for peer firms. The literature, however, shows that experienced analysts can miss relevant financial information due to attention constraints (e.g.,Abarbanell and Bushee 1997;Teoh and Wong 2002;Harford et al. 2019;Kumar, Rantala, and Xu 2022), suggesting that analysts could also fail to pay attention to information disclosed in merger documents.

We construct three forecast variables to test analyst attention: consensus analyst earnings forecast (EPS), consensus analyst recommendation (Rec), and consensus analyst price target forecast (PTG). For analyst recommendations, the Institutional Brokers’ Estimate System, or I/B/E/S, assigns a one to “strong buy” and a five to “sell”; thus, recommendations worsen as the number increases.Table 9, panel A, presents the regression results. For the nontarget subsample, the significantly negative coefficient forPeerin column 1 suggests that analysts lower their forecasts for peer firms’ earnings relative to control firms around the filing month. Moreover, columns 2 and 3 show that they also adjust downward their recommendations and price target forecasts for peer firms. Columns 4 to 6 report results for the target subsample, where we find insignificant coefficients forPeerfor all three forecast variables.

The results reported inTable 9, panel A, suggest that analysts are more pessimistic about peer firms around the filing month, particularly for the nontarget subsample. To investigate whether analysts are unduly pessimistic about peer firms, we examine ex post analyst forecast error and earnings announcement returns. We compute forecast error as the difference between the actual quarterly earnings and the premerger filing consensus forecast. We also calculate the 3-day abnormal returns surrounding the earnings announcement date. Panel B ofTable 9reports the results. We find that for the nontarget subsample, both forecast errors and earnings CARs are higher for peer firms, indicating that analysts underestimate earnings for peers. For the target subsample, analyst forecast errors are insignificant, and earnings CARs are higher for peer firms. The results in panel B suggest that analysts underestimate earnings for peers.

Why are analysts more pessimistic about peers around the filing month? We explore this counterintuitive finding by testing earnings CARs and quarterly stock returns right before the merger filing month. Prior studies show that analysts’ forecast revisions are related to recent stock price performance, particularly recent earnings announcement returns (e.g.,Lys and Sohn 1990;Abarbanell 1991;Clement, Hales, and Xue 2011). As shown in panel C ofFigure 2, peers’ stock price performance is lower than that of control firms before the filing month. If analysts incorporate this information in revising their earnings forecasts, they would be more pessimistic about peer firms’ future earnings. We investigate this potential explanation more formally inTable IA5in theInternet Appendix. We find that peers have lower earnings CARs and price performance in the quarter prior to the filing month. Moreover, the results are concentrated among the nontarget subsample. These results suggest that analysts miss the peer information in merger documents and rely on recent stock performance and earnings announcement returns to guide their forecast revisions and recommendations, leading to more pessimistic revisions for peers.

5.5 Additional investigation on investor attention

In this subsection, we conduct two additional analyses of investor attention. First,Peng and Xiong (2006)predict that the return predictive power of an ignored signal about a firm decreases with the amount of firm-specific information processed by the investor. Their argument is that the information value of an ignored signal depends on how much information investors have already processed from other sources, which in turn is determined by the attention that investors allocate to the firm. In our setting, this argument predicts that if investors are already paying enough attention to the peer firm, then the information content disclosed in the merger document is reduced, which lowers subsequent alphas. We thus use abnormal turnover to measure investor attention and rerun our calendar portfolio tests separately for selected peers with high abnormal turnover versus low abnormal turnover.

Table IA6in theInternet Appendixpresents the set of results. Consistent with the prediction ofPeng and Xiong (2006), the portfolio that longs selected peers with low abnormal turnover and shorts matched control firms earns a statistically significant 0.58%alpha (⁠t=5.35⁠) per month over the following 12-month period based on the six-factor model, while the corresponding portfolio that longs peers with high abnormal turnover and shorts matched control firms earns a statistically significant 0.32%alpha (⁠t=3.16⁠) per month over the following 12-month period based on the six-factor model. These results further support the investor inattention explanation.

Second, we calculate changes in institutional ownership around merger filing to investigate institutional investors’ trading behavior right after the release of peer information. Specifically, we calculate the institutional ownership change from the quarter immediately before to after the merger filing date. We report the results inTable IA7in theInternet Appendix. We find that around merger filing, nontarget peers experience an increase in institutional ownership, indicating that some investors purchase peer firms to capture future abnormal returns. However, relative to control firms, the increase in institutional ownership is only 0.2%⁠, which is very small given thatTable 6shows peers experiencing an increase in institutional ownership of over 3%in the subsequent years. Our interpretation is that although a small number of institutional investors are buying peers following the merger filing, the majority may have initially missed the information, leading to delayed price responses.
article.6. Additional Analyses and Robustness Ch:
We conduct additional analyses and robustness tests in this section.

6.1 Advisor selection when peers later become takeover targets

Why do peers chosen by an M&A advisor as comparable firms have a much higher probability of becoming subsequent target firms themselves? We conjecture that two potential channels can contribute to this effect. One, a more direct channel, would be that investment banks may actively facilitate later transactions when peers themselves become targets. That is, a bank may actively solicit business from the peer firm and try to persuade its management to sell. The cost of doing so is low because the advisor has already researched the peer firm’s financial conditions. It is also possible that when a potential acquirer hires a bank to search for a potential target, the bank may be more likely to recommend one of the peers it identified in its earlier M&A valuation analysis. To detect the role that investment banks play in later takeover transactions, we investigate the likelihood of them becoming advisors in the later M&A deals by estimating the following regression model: Deal advisor = β 0 + β 1 IB selectedthe target as a peer + Controls + ε . (2)

For this analysis, we form an M&A sample where peer firms later become takeover targets. For each takeover transaction, we identify its bidder-side and target-side investment banks. For each investment bank, we randomly select five other banks from the bank population in our sample as pseudo-advisors. The dependent variable is eitherTarget advisor, an indicator variable that equals one if an investment bank is hired by the target firm, orBidder advisor, an indicator variable that equals one if an investment bank is hired by the bidder. The main independent variable isIB selected the target as a peer, an indicator variable that equals one if an investment bank selected the target firm as a peer in the past for other M&A valuation analyses.27

Table 10presents the regression results. The coefficients forIB selected the target as a peerare significantly positive in all model specifications, suggesting that an investment bank that previously selected the target firm as a peer firm is more likely to be hired by the target or the bidder. The positive effect on advisor selection is not only statistically significant but also economically meaningful. The coefficient for the main independent variable in column 3 is 0.184, indicating an 18.4%increase in the probability of being hired by the target if the investment bank selected the target as a peer previously. 6.2 Advisor informational advantage

Another possibility is that investment banks may have more information, which makes their peer selection informative. During their valuation process, banks are granted access to confidential information about the target firm, which enables bank advisors to better identify comparable firms than other market participants who have access to public information only. This information advantage becomes particularly important when evaluating target firms that are more complex, have greater information asymmetry, and have less publicly available data. The information channel suggests that peers selected in deals with high information asymmetry will have a higher likelihood of becoming a target relative to their control firms.

Following prior studies (e.g.,Aboody and Lev 2000;Cohen and Lou 2012;Ellis, Fee, and Thomas 2012), we measure firms’ public information environment using three proxies: research and development (R&D) expenditures, an indicator variable that equals one if a target firm has multiple business or geographic segments, and an indicator variable that equals one if a target has foreign business operations. We rerun regressions inTable 2by including interaction terms betweenPeerand three proxies of information asymmetry, respectively. For brevity, we only report results for regressions of Target_1Y and Target_3Y.

Table 11shows that the coefficients for the interaction terms are significantly positive for all models. Because we include deal fixed effects, the regressions estimatewithindeal parameters. The coefficient for the interaction term captures whether target information asymmetry has a mitigating or enforcing effect on peers becoming a target relative to control firms within-deal. The results suggest that peers are more likely to become targets than control firms when the deal involves a target with high information asymmetry.29

6.3 Cross-sectional analysis

The mechanism discussed in the previous section indicates that some peer firms may experience even higher returns if, ex ante, we expect them to have a higher probability of becoming targets. In this subsection, we conduct cross-sectional tests to investigate this possibility.

Prior studies have shown that the probability of being acquired is higher when firm size is smaller (Hasbrouck 1985;Palepu 1986).Gorbenko and Malenko (2014)argue that different target firms appeal to different types of bidders. The authors find that takeover competition is much higher among private cash bidders because valuation creation through acquiring these types of target firms is less likely to be bidder specific. In those deals, competing private cash bidders that lost the auction for the target firm may try to acquire the peers. As a result, selected peers that are smaller in size or in deals with private cash bidders may have an increased probability of becoming targets. We display evidence consistent with this supposition inTable IA9in theInternet Appendix.

In panel A ofTable 12, we conduct calendar portfolio tests for a subsample of small peers and for deals involving private cash bidders. Consistent with our expectations, we find that the long-short portfolio in the subsample of small peers generates alphas ranging from 0.55%to 0.58%per month over the following 12-month period. The long-short portfolio in deals with private cash bidders earns alphas ranging from 0.63%to 0.81%per month.
