6.4 The role of product market space

In this subsection, we explore the role of product market space in explaining peers becoming takeover targets subsequently. We separate peer firms into two groups based on whether they are in the same product market space as the target firm. We use text-based network industry classification (TNIC2) codes, which derive from the product similarity scores ofHoberg and Phillips (2010,2016)in order to measure product market space.30We further form two control groups based on the control firms’ product market spaces. The first control group includes nonpeer firms that are in the same product market space as the target. The second control group includes nonpeer firms that are from other product market spaces than that of the target firm. We keep the size restrictions so that each control firm’s size is similar to the matched peer firm.

Hoberg and Phillips (2010)find that M&A transactions are more likely to occur between firms that produce similar products based on their 10-K business descriptions, suggesting that peers in the same product market space as the target firm could have an even higher probability of becoming targets compared with peers from different product market spaces. The increased takeover probability could produce higher subsequent abnormal returns. However, these peers from the same product market space also can be more easily identified by the market, which makes them more likely to be expected peers. Because of this expectation effect, these peers’ stock prices could have already incorporated the higher likelihood of becoming takeover targets, which reduces subsequent abnormal returns. Therefore, the role of product market space in explaining peers’ subsequent abnormal returns is unclear ex ante.

Panel A ofTable IA10in theInternet Appendixreports summary statistics for the likelihood of the four groups becoming targets. We find that, consistent with our expectation, peers that are in the same product market space as the target have a significantly higher probability of becoming targets in the subsequent 1 to 3 years, ranging from 9%to over 20%⁠, compared with peers from different product market spaces, with a takeover probability ranging from 6%to 15%⁠. Results for the two control groups are also interesting. Nonpeers from different product market spaces have the lowest probability of becoming targets among all groups, ranging from only 2%to 5%⁠. Nonpeer firms from the same product market space as the target have a relatively high probability of becoming targets, ranging from 6%to 14%⁠. These numbers are similar to peers from different product market spaces but still lower than those from the same product market space.

Panel B ofTable IA10in theInternet Appendixreports the results of calendar-time portfolios for the four groups. Peers from the same product market space earn the largest alphas. This result suggests that the market underestimates return performance for peers from the same product market space. Peers from different product market spaces also earn positive alphas, albeit with smaller magnitudes. Interestingly, despite the high probability of becoming targets, nonpeers from the same product market space generally do not earn abnormal alphas, suggesting that, for this group, the anticipated takeover probability has already been reflected in their stock prices, resulting in no subsequent abnormal returns. Finally, nonpeers from different product market spaces generally earn negative but insignificant alphas, with the exception of our using the three-factor model, which produces a negative and significant alpha at the 10%level.

Overall, the results presented inTable IA10in theInternet Appendixsuggest that the product market space is important in explaining M&A activities, as the probability of becoming a target is relatively high for both peers and nonpeers from the same product market space as the target firm. However, the large alphas for peers and the insignificant alphas for nonpeers from the same product market space suggest that the observable product market or industry effect cannot fully explain our peer results. It appears that investment banks’ advantages in selecting peers go beyond publicly disclosed product and business descriptions, consistent with our earlier analysis showing that banks have informational advantages and deal-facilitation ability. Moreover, the results suggest that while the market is able to anticipate the higher takeover probability for nonpeers from the same product market space, it fails to recognize the incremental value implication associated with being selected as a peer by investment banks, which leads to subsequent abnormal returns.

6.5 Robustness check: Alternative control firms based on propensity score matching

To further alleviate concerns that our results are driven by different firm characteristics, we implement a propensity score matching technique as a robustness test. We first construct a pool of potential control firms with the same two-digit SIC code and of a size within 50%to 150%of the peer firm in each deal. We include control variables fromTable 2, panel B, and a four-digit SIC fixed effect as predictive variables in the first-stage model. We then match, without replacement, a company that is not selected as a peer firm with the closest propensity score after setting the maximum caliper width of 0.01 within each deal. We rerun our main analysis using the propensity-score-matched control firms and report the results inTable IA11in theInternet Appendix. We continue to find that peers significantly outperform, suggesting that our results are unlikely to be driven by different firm characteristics.

6.6 Robustness check: Excluding tender offers

As discussed in Section2.1, disclosure requirements in M&As depend on the form of the deal. Because the disclosure of valuation analyses in fairness opinions is required only when firms issue a proxy solicitation to approve the deal, target firms in a tender offer are not required to disclose the valuation analyses in the associated filings, as a target shareholder vote is not required in tender offers. Our inclusion of deal fixed effects alleviates concerns that the selective disclosure of valuation analysis in tender offers could affect our results in most analyses. As a further robustness check, we rerun our main analysis excluding tender offers and report the results inTable IA12in theInternet Appendix. We continue to find that peers significantly outperform the control firms.
article.7. Conclusion:
Using a novel data source taken from firms’ merger documents filed with the SEC, we show that being selected as a peer firm comparable to a target firm in an M&A valuation analysis provided by investment banks has important implications for the peers’ future corporate outcomes and stock returns. Being identified as a peer is associated with a significantly higher likelihood of becoming a takeover target in the next few years. Peer firms not becoming targets attract more institutional ownership and coverage, deliver strong operating performance, reduce investments, and increase payouts.

However, investors are inattentive to the identification of peers at the time they are publicly disclosed in merger documents. We find no price reactions around the merger filing date. Yet a portfolio formed at the end of the filing month that shorts nonpeers from the same industry and longs peers earns 43 to 46 basis points per month over the following year. The returns continue to accrue out to 24 months and do not reverse, suggesting that these return movements are not overreactions. Using merger filing downloads from EDGAR and news search from Bloomberg as proxies for investor attention, we find that peer firms do not receive abnormal attention when merger filings are made public. Security analysts also seem to miss the peer information when revising their forecasts around the filing month.

Our interpretation of these results is that, owing to the nature of M&A events and the length and complexity of merger filings, inattentive investors miss valuable information contained in merger filings that has implications for firm valuation changes. Because of this inattention, the change in firm value is gradually incorporated into asset prices over a long period of time after public disclosure of the information. We provide novel evidence suggesting that investment banks’ valuation analyses in takeover deals contain valuable information that the capital market appears to ignore.

OPINION OF THE COMPANY’S FINANCIAL ADVISOR

Pursuant to letter agreements dated August 25, 1999 and March 3, 2000, the Company formally retained Gordian to act as its financial advisor in connection with one or more financial transactions, including a possible acquisition, sale, merger, joint venture or business combination, and to render an opinion (the “Opinion”) to the Board of Directors regarding the fairness, from a financial point of view, to the holders of Company Common Stock of the cash consideration (the “Consideration”) to be received by the holders of Company Common Stock in the Transaction.

At the meeting of the Board of Directors held on April 11, 2000, Gordian delivered to the Company board an oral Opinion, which opinion was subsequently confirmed by delivery of a written Opinion dated April 11, 2000, stating that the Consideration to be received in the Offer and the Merger was fair, from a financial point of view, to the holders of Company Common Stock. In arriving at the Opinion, over a period of months prior to and leading up to the April 11, 2000 meeting of the Board of Directors, Gordian (i) reviewed the drafts dated April 10, 2000 of the Merger Agreement, License Agreement, and Stock Option Agreement, (ii) reviewed certain publicly available information concerning the Company including the Annual Reports on Form 10-K for the years ended 1996, 1997, 1998 and 1999 and the Quarterly Reports on Form 10- Q of the Company for the quarters ended March, June and September 1999, (iii) reviewed certain operating and internal information of the Company, provided to Gordian by the management of the Company, including financial projections for various periods ended December 31, 2004, under a variety of assumptions, including both with and without giving effect to the Offer and the Merger, (iv) reviewed certain internal analyses concerning alternatives to the Offer and the Merger, provided to Gordian by the management of the Company, (v) reviewed certain publicly available information with respect to certain other companies that Gordian believed to be comparable, in certain respects, to the Company, (vi) reviewed certain publicly available information concerning the nature and terms of certain other transactions that Gordian considered relevant to its inquiry, and (vii) met (in person or telephonically) with certain officers and employees of the Company and with its legal advisors to discuss aspects of the foregoing as well as the business, prospects, future cash flows, forecasts and operating condition of the Company.

In arriving at the Opinion, Gordian performed a variety of financial analyses, the material portions of which are summarized below. This summary does not purport to be a complete description of the analyses performed by Gordian. In addition, Gordian’s analyses must be considered as a whole and selecting portions of its analysis or the factors considered by it, without considering all such factors and analyses, could create a misleading view of the process underlying its analyses. Gordian did not draw any specific conclusions from or with regard to any one method of analysis in isolation. Arriving at a fairness opinion is a complex process not necessarily susceptible to partial or summary description. The matters considered by Gordian in arriving at the Opinion are based on numerous macroeconomic, operating and financial assumptions with respect to industry performance, general business, economic and market conditions and other matters, many of which are beyond the Company’s control. The analyses performed by Gordian are not necessarily indicative of actual values, which may be significantly more or less favorable than suggested by such analyses. Because such analyses are inherently subject to uncertainty, none of the Company or Gordian or any other person assumes responsibility if future events do not conform to the judgments reflected in the Opinion.

In rendering the Opinion, Gordian assumed and relied, without independent verification, upon the accuracy and completeness of all financial and other information publicly available or furnished to or otherwise reviewed by or discussed with Gordian as well as certain representations from the Company and its management. With respect to financial forecasts and other information provided to or otherwise reviewed by or discussed with Gordian, the management of the Company advised Gordian that such forecasts were reasonably prepared on bases reflecting the best currently available estimates and judgments of the management of the Company as to the future financial performance of the Company and the forecasts and other information were true, complete and accurate in all material respects and not misleading. Gordian did not make or obtain an independent evaluation or appraisal of the assets, or reserves of the Company nor did Gordian make any physical inspection of the properties or assets of the Company.

The Opinion dated April 11, 2000, which sets forth the assumptions made, matters considered and limitations on the review undertaken, is attached hereto as Annex D and is incorporated herein by reference. Company shareholders are urged to read the Opinion carefully in its entirety. The Opinion is directed only to the fairness of the Consideration to the holders of Company Common Stock from a financial point of view. The Opinion was furnished solely to the Board of Directors and is not to be relied upon by any other person, including any shareholder, creditor of the Company or other interested party. The summary of the Opinion of Gordian set forth in this Proxy Statement is qualified in its entirety by reference to the full text of such Opinion.

The following is a summary of the analyses prepared by Gordian and presented by Gordian to the Company and the Board of Directors in a series of meetings preceding and including the April 11, 2000 meeting at which Gordian delivered the Opinion:

Stock Trading History. Gordian reviewed the history of the trading prices of the Company Common Stock during the period from and including April 7, 1995 to April 10, 2000.

Comparable Company Analysis. Gordian analyzed certain publicly available financial information, operating data and projected financial performance (per research analyst estimates) of the followinggroup of six publicly traded ice cream and frozen yogurt companiesGordian believed to be appropriate for comparison with that of the Company:Dreyer’s Grand Ice Cream, Inc., Eskimo Pie Corp., Friendly Ice Cream Corp., TCBY Enterprises, Inc., Yocream International and Yogen Fruz World-Wide Inc.

Comparable Acquisition Analysis. Gordian also reviewed the consideration paid or proposed to be paid in other acquisitions of companies in the food industry. Specifically, Gordian analyzed the financial terms, to the extent publicly available, of over 80 transactions (some of which were not consummated) involving food industry companies that were announced since December 1996.

Premiums Paid Analysis. Gordian determined the premium over market price paid for publicly traded companies in over 20 selected transactions announced since December 1996.

Other Analyses. Gordian performed a variety of additional analyses, including but not limited to, recapitalization analyses and purchase analysis at various prices and other sensitivity analyses.

No company or transaction used in the comparable company analysis or comparable acquisition analysis summarized above is identical to the Company or the Offer and the Merger. Accordingly, any such analysis of the fairness of the Consideration involves complex considerations and judgments concerning differences in the potential financial and operating characteristics of the comparable companies and other factors in relation to the trading, premiums and acquisition values of the comparable companies and publicly announced transactions.

The Board of Directors retained Gordian based upon Gordian’s qualifications, experience and expertise. Gordian is a nationally recognized investment banking and advisory firm. Gordian, as part of its investment banking and financial advisory business, is continuously engaged in the valuation of businesses and securities in connection with various transactions and for corporate and other purposes. Gordian and one of its officers held equity securities of the Company, including Company Common Stock and a warrant to purchase shares of Company Common Stock. Gordian has had, and continues to have, significant business relationships with Mr. Odak. Moreover, in the ordinary course of business, Gordian has had or may have dealings with certain other holders of the Company’s securities, in matters unrelated to the Company. Gordian has and has had numerous clients in a wide variety of industries. It is possible that some of these past or present Gordian clients may have some connection to the Company or to holders of its securities.

5 Merger documents are filed with the SEC a few weeks following the deal’s announcement and before the deal’s completion. Typically, a PREM/DEFM 14A (and its amendment) is filed by the target firm in a cash deal. For a stock deal, an S-4 (and its amendment) is filed by either the bidder or jointly. For a tender offer, an SC14D9/SC 14D1 is filed by the target firm. Note that bidder shareholders are typically not required to approve the deal; thus, no proxy statement needs to be filed by the bidders in most cases. Bidder shareholder approval is required only if a bidder needs to issue 20 % or more new shares to finance the deal. See appendix IA1 in Li, Liu, and Wu (2018) for more information on the listing rules of the New York Stock Exchange, American Stock Exchange, and Nasdaq and on shareholder approval in M&As.

6 Using a sample of over 3,000 mergers from 2006 to 2016, Duff & Phelps show that the most common valuation method was CCA, followed by DCF ( Bartell and Janssen 2017 ).

7 See DeAngelo (1990) , Davidoff (2006) , Cain and Denis (2013) , and Liu (2020) for more details on disclosure rules and fairness opinions. Because of the shifting nature of Delaware courts’ requirements for fairness opinion disclosure in tender offers, we do observe that, over time, more firms have chosen to disclose detailed valuation information in tender offers: from 10.9 % in the first half of our sample period to 75.3 % in the second half of our sample period.

8 Selecting overpriced peers for negotiation might also increase investment banks’ litigation risks, although it is typically difficult to hold investment banks liable in connection with their provision of a fairness opinion ( Davidoff 2006 ).

9 Our sample period starts in 1994, when the first-year merger documents were disclosed in the SEC’s EDGAR, although not all firms were required to have a filing disclosed in 1994. See Gao and Huang (2020) for a history of the implementation of EDGAR.

10 In U.S. M&A transactions, the target firm needs to file a PREM/DEFM 14A for a cash offer or an SC 14D1/SC 14D9 (and its amendment) for a tender offer or a joint filing S-4 (and its amendment) for a stock offer. We are unable to find merger documents for some of the deals in our sample for two main reasons. First, most withdrawn deals never reached a definitive merger agreement and thus had no merger documents. Second, the gradual phase-in schedule before 1996 means not all firms filed merger documents with EDGAR in the early years.

11 Our results are not affected if we exclude tender offers from our sample.

12 We have 39,114 peer observations before we merge the peer sample with CRSP. We lose 3,827 observations after merging with CRSP due to some peers being traded on the over-the-counter market and international peers being traded on foreign exchanges. We discuss these situations in more detail in Appendix IA1 .

13 Specifically, we search for matching nonpeers from the same two-digit industry group and with a market capitalization between 50 % and 150 % of the peer firm’s market capitalization. We use SIC codes from Compustat. Among these nonpeers, we choose the one with the smallest size difference. We drop the peer firm if we are not able to identify a control firm that satisfies these requirements. As a robustness check, we remove the size restriction and still find robust results.

14 We include deal fixed effects to control for unobservable deal characteristics. Because adding a large number of fixed effects to a traditional probit or logit model would induce incidental parameter bias, we follow Lancaster (2000) and Angrist (2001) and use a linear probability model to estimate marginal effects. In panel A of Table IA1 in the Internet Appendix , we reestimate Equation ( 1 ) using a conditional logit model with deal fixed effects. We present the odds ratios (at the bottom of the table) to facilitate the interpretation of the economic magnitude. We find that, consistent with the results from using ordinary least-squares models, the odds for peer firms becoming a takeover target are about 100.8 % (83.5 % and 85 % ⁠ ) higher than the odds for nonpeer firms in the next 12 (24 or 36) months. In panel B of Table IA1 in the Internet Appendix , we reestimate our tests with a probit model and obtain a similar inference.

15 It is possible that a control firm later becomes a peer firm, in this case, we remove the control firm observations (only after it becomes a peer firm).

16 In our main analysis, we report the heteroscedastic-robust standard errors. In panel A of Table IA2 in the Internet Appendix , we cluster standard errors at the firm level to account for potential serial correlation in the error terms for repeated firms (peer or control) in our sample. In panel B of Table IA2 in the Internet Appendix , we cluster standard errors at the deal level. Overall, we find robust results.

17 Eckbo (1983) , Fee and Thomas (2004) , and Shahrur (2005) also show that the target firm’s industry peers positively react to deal announcements.

18 In unreported results, we extend the period up to 3 years (i.e., 36 months following the release of the merger documents) and find that the return differences continue to widen instead of reverting.

19 Our results remain robust if we use value-weighted returns, although the magnitude of alphas is smaller.

20 As a further robustness test, we employ Fama and MacBeth (1973) regression analysis to control for more firm characteristics in Table IA3 in the Internet Appendix . We find that the coefficient for the key independent variable, Peer , is positive and highly significant.

21 We acknowledge that short-selling constraints are not the only “limit to arbitrage.” In unreported analysis, we investigate how idiosyncratic risk, a proxy for arbitrage risk (e.g., Pontiff 2006 ), affects the peer effect. We find that the calendar-time portfolio returns are similar among peers with low or high idiosyncratic volatility.

22 In unreported results, we test whether peers remaining independent experience a price reversal during the second year (i.e., from months 13 to 24) and find no evidence of reversal.

23 As a robustness check, we also construct the measure following Richardson, Sloan, and You (2012) and find similar results.

24 We follow Loughran and McDonald (2017) and define internet protocols (IPs) with more than 50 downloads in a day as being robot activity. Our results are similar when we follow Drake, Roulstone, and Thornock (2015) , Ryans (2017) , or Lee, Ma, and Wang (2015) in eliminating the robot downloads.

25 We are able to conduct this analysis for the deals filed between February 14, 2003, and June 30, 2017, since the SEC only made internet search traffic for EDGAR filings available through SEC.gov in this period. The original data can be downloaded from https://www.sec.gov/dera/data/edgar-log-file-data-set.html .

26 Specifically, a score of zero indicates that the hourly search activity on day t is in the lowest 80 % of the hourly counts over the previous 30 days; a score of four indicates the hourly search activity is greater than 96 % of the previous 30 days’ hourly counts.

27 The literature suggests that past business relations and bank reputation matter in the investment banking business (e.g., Fang 2005 ; Ljungqvist, Marston, and Wilhelm 2006 ); we therefore include proxies for prior business relations that the banks may have had with the target or the bidder and a proxy for banks’ reputation as control variables. For some regression models, we further include deal fixed and advisor fixed effects to control for unobservable deal and bank characteristics.

28 Eaton, Liu, and Officer (2021) document that the private merger negotiation process from deal initiation to public announcement can take 3 to 4 months. Given the lengthy process, one might be concerned that the investment bank could simultaneously advise the target firm and one of the peer firms, which could explain the higher likelihood of investment banks being hired later. To address this concern, in untabulated results, we exclude transactions with peers that become targets within 6 months following previous M&A transactions in which those peers were identified. Our results remain robust.

29 To investigate potential cross-sectional peer effects, we remove deal fixed effects and report coefficients for both the interaction term and the first-order term in Table IA8 in the Internet Appendix . The coefficients for the first-order term of our proxies for information asymmetry are negative and significant, suggesting that control firms in complex deals have a reduced likelihood of becoming targets. To compare peers of complex targets with peers of noncomplex targets, we add the coefficients for the interaction term and the first-order term together and test the sum of the coefficients. Peers of targets with more segments or foreign operations generally have a lower likelihood of becoming targets, while peers of R&D intensive targets have a similar probability of becoming targets compared with peers of non-R&D-intensive targets.

30 We thank Professors Gerard Hoberg and Gordon Phillips for making their data publicly available. Hoberg and Phillips (2010 , 2016) define TNIC2 as two firms having pairwise product similarities above a threshold, as a means of refining the coarseness of the two-digit SIC classification. The threshold is the ex ante probability of two randomly drawn firms from the CRSP/Compustat universe having the same two-digit SIC code. Our results are similar if we use a three-digit text-based network industry classification.
